summary: Alfred is a text editor with no database backend. The data layer is in-memory: text is stored in a rope data structure (ropey crate), cursor/viewport state in Rust structs, and undo/redo in memory snapshots. File I/O is handled via std::fs and std::io. There are no database schema migrations, ORM usage, SQL queries, transactions, or connection pooling. Database-focused assessment criteria are not applicable.

migration_health.notes: No database migrations. Text editor uses in-memory buffer (rope) and file-based persistence.

data_layer_architecture.notes: Pure in-memory data model with file save/load. No concurrent access control needed (single-threaded TUI). Rope enables efficient text operations (insert, delete, substring) without O(n) buffer copies.

findings.title: Rope data structure for buffer management
findings.description: Buffer uses ropey crate (rope data structure). This is efficient for text editing (logarithmic operation complexity) and enables cheap undo/redo via structural sharing. Snapshots are cloned before each edit, allowing reversion.

findings.title: Shared mutable state via Rc<RefCell<T>>
findings.description: EditorState is wrapped in Rc<RefCell<EditorState>> for shared mutable access across Lisp runtime and event loop. This is a common pattern in Rust TUI apps where multiple subsystems need to mutate state. No concurrent threads, so RefCell's runtime borrow checking is sufficient.

findings.title: File I/O error handling
findings.description: File read/write errors are wrapped in thiserror enums (FileReadError, FileWriteError) with path and source context. Follows domain-driven error handling pattern.

recommendations.title: Assessment not applicable
recommendations.description: Data layer assessment criteria (migrations, ORM, N+1 queries, transactions, connection pools) are designed for applications with persistent databases. Alfred uses in-memory data structures and file-based persistence. A separate audit for in-memory data structure efficiency, undo/redo correctness, and file I/O error handling would be more appropriate.

recommendations.title: Document rope data structure usage
recommendations.description: Create a DATA_ARCHITECTURE.md documenting why ropey is used, how text operations are implemented, and performance characteristics (O(log n) for insert/delete). This aids future maintainers.

recommendations.title: Consider concurrent editing (future work)
recommendations.description: If Alfred ever supports collaborative editing or multi-threaded operations, the Rc<RefCell<T>> pattern will need revision to use Arc<Mutex<T>> or RwLock<T>. Document this architectural constraint.

recommendations.title: Test undo/redo snapshot correctness with large files
recommendations.description: Rope cloning is cheap, but verify that undo/redo snapshots are correctly restored for large files (> 1 MB). Add property-based tests for undo/redo invariants.

recommendations.title: Review file encoding handling
recommendations.description: File I/O assumes UTF-8. Verify that non-UTF-8 files are handled gracefully (e.g., display error, offer alternative encodings). Add tests for binary files and mixed encodings.
